planting.note: Frost-tender climber grown warm indoors.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting against a sturdy pole in a coarse, well-drained aroid mix. The large heart-shaped leaves develop subtle silvery patterning and warty petioles as the vine matures in bright indirect light. Keep evenly moist during growth, feed monthly, and maintain steady warmth and humidity.

planting.note: Frost-tender species grown as a warm houseplant.

directions: Root a stem cutting and grow it on a pole in a free-draining aroid mix. The long, narrow, arrow-shaped leaves are green above with a striking maroon underside, best appreciated in bright indirect light. Water when the top inch dries and keep it warm and out of cold drafts.

planting.note: Extremely rare frost-tender collector species grown warm and humid indoors.

directions: Grow this prized rarity on a moss pole in a very airy, well-drained mix with high humidity. The pendant, elongated triangular leaves hang gracefully and enlarge slowly as the plant climbs in bright indirect light. Water when the top inch dries, keep warmth steady, and handle the slow-growing plant gently.

planting.note: Compact frost-tender hybrid grown as a small warm-room houseplant.

directions: Pot this dwarf, self-heading hybrid in a well-drained mix and place it in bright indirect light. Its small, glossy, wavy-edged leaves form a tight rosette perfect for a desk or shelf. Water when the top inch dries and feed lightly in the growing season; no staking is needed.

planting.note: Frost-tender climber for warm, humid interiors or frost-free gardens.

directions: Root a node cutting and train it up a stout moss pole in a chunky, well-drained aroid mix. Mature leaves develop dramatic, lacy fenestrations that nearly reach the margins, giving the skeletal look behind its name. Provide bright indirect light and steady humidity, watering when the top inch dries.

planting.note: Frost-tender climbing species grown as a warm houseplant.

directions: Root a cutting and give it a pole to climb in a bark-rich, well-drained mix. Juvenile stems press flat against the support in a shingling habit before larger leaves with modest fenestrations develop higher up. Keep it warm and humid in bright indirect light, watering when the surface dries.

planting.note: Frost-tender climber grown warm indoors or in frost-free gardens.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting against a moss pole in a coarse, free-draining mix. The oval leaves develop rows of small oval holes near the midrib rather than large splits as they mature. Give it bright indirect light and steady humidity, watering when the top inch of the mix dries.

planting.note: Frost-tender climbing species for warm, humid interiors.

directions: Root a cutting and provide a slender pole in an airy, well-drained aroid mix. Unlike its holey relatives, this species has fully divided, feathery leaflets that give a delicate palm-like appearance. Keep it in bright indirect light with good humidity and water when the top inch dries.

planting.note: Frost-tender climbing species grown warm and humid indoors.

directions: Set a rooted cutting on a moss pole in a chunky, free-draining mix. Its leathery leaves develop lobed edges and scattered fenestrations as the vine climbs and matures. Provide bright indirect light and consistent humidity, watering when the top inch of the mix dries and feeding monthly in the growing season.

planting.note: Pot up indoors any time in spring once warmth is steady.

directions: Grow this pendant epiphyte in a loose bark-and-perlite mix in a hanging basket so the long ribbon leaves can trail freely. Keep the medium lightly moist and give it warm, humid air away from direct sun. It matures slowly, and the beaded pink infructescences signal a happy, well-established plant.

planting.note: Start in warm indoor conditions during the spring flush.

directions: Mount or pot this velvet strap-leaf epiphyte in coarse, fast-draining media and hang it where the leaves can cascade. It demands high humidity and steady moisture without soggy roots. Look for the corkscrew-curled spadix, which is the species' signature bloom.

planting.note: Establish new divisions in spring warmth.

directions: This pendant strap-leaf species has thick, velvety leaves and prefers a chunky epiphyte mix in a hanging pot. Water when the top layer begins to dry and keep humidity high year round. Give it filtered light to protect the deep-green foliage from scorching.

planting.note: Pot divisions once spring temperatures stabilize.

directions: Plant this narrow strap-leaf anthurium in a bark-rich, freely draining mix and keep it in bright, indirect light. Its arching leaves are followed by showy strings of bright red berries, its main ornamental feature. Water regularly but let excess drain, since standing water rots the roots.

planting.note: Best potted up in spring warmth.

directions: This small epiphytic anthurium thrives in a very open, airy mix and appreciates high humidity. It is grown chiefly for its dangling clusters of glossy red berries that resemble strung pearls. Keep it lightly moist and shaded from harsh midday sun.

planting.note: Move divisions into warm indoor conditions in spring.

directions: Prized for its bullate, quilted leaves with an almost metallic sheen, this species wants a chunky aroid mix and steady warmth. Keep humidity high and let the top of the medium dry slightly between waterings. Protect the textured foliage from direct sun, which dulls and burns the leaf surface.

planting.note: Pot in spring as growth resumes.

directions: This bird's-nest-type anthurium forms a rosette of stiff, leathery paddle-shaped leaves and needs a coarse, well-drained mix. Water when the surface dries and give it bright, filtered light. Its upright form makes a bold accent once the leaves reach full size.

planting.note: Start in warm, humid conditions in spring.

directions: A climbing anthurium with palmately compound leaves divided into finger-like leaflets, best grown up a moss pole. Keep it in a loose, airy mix with bright, indirect light and consistent humidity. Water when the top inch dries and provide support as the vining stems lengthen.

planting.note: Divide and repot in spring.

directions: This robust bird's-nest anthurium builds a large rosette of broad, leathery leaves and tolerates a range of indoor conditions. Use a coarse, well-drained mix and water when the surface dries. Slender purple-red spadices rise among the leaves once the plant is mature.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring warmth.

directions: Named for its palmately divided leaves that resemble cannabis foliage, this vining anthurium climbs readily on a moss support. Grow it in an open aroid mix with bright, indirect light and even moisture. Pinch or train the stems to keep the plant full and upright.

planting.note: Pot divisions in spring.

directions: This velvety-leaved species shows off pale silver veining against dark green blades and prefers a chunky, well-aerated mix. Keep humidity high and water when the surface dries slightly. Shield the soft foliage from direct sun to preserve its velvet texture.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring warmth.

directions: A small climbing epiphyte, this anthurium is grown for its clusters of translucent white to lavender berries. Mount it on bark or pot it in an airy mix and keep humidity high. Water when the top layer dries and give it bright, indirect light.

planting.note: Repot and divide in spring.

directions: This velvet-leaved hybrid produces broad, heart-shaped blades so dark green they appear nearly black. Grow it in a loose, chunky mix with high humidity and bright, filtered light. Let the surface dry slightly between waterings and keep the foliage out of direct sun.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring as growth restarts.

directions: This forgiving foliage plant grows in a standard peaty houseplant mix in low to moderate light. Water when the top inch dries and avoid cold drafts, which spot the leaves. Divide crowded clumps at repotting to make new plants.

planting.note: Repot and divide in spring.

directions: A low, compact species with a white midrib and cream speckling across dark green leaves. Grow it in a rich, well-drained mix and keep it evenly moist but never soggy. It prefers warm, humid conditions and shade from direct sun.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring.

directions: This vigorous cultivar has large leaves splashed with broad silvery-green centers. Grow it in a peaty, free-draining mix and water when the top inch dries. It tolerates lower light well, making it an easy office or living-room plant.

planting.note: Repot and divide in spring.

directions: One of the most shade-tolerant Chinese evergreens, with dark green leaves finely marbled in silvery gray. Grow it in a standard houseplant mix and let the top layer dry before watering. Keep it warm and out of direct sun for the best leaf color.

planting.note: Divide and repot in spring.

directions: This colorful cultivar has green leaves edged and veined in bright pink to red. Give it brighter indirect light than the plain green types to keep the red vivid. Water when the top inch dries and keep it warm and draft-free.

planting.note: Repot in spring warmth.

directions: A compact species with near-black rounded leaves crossed by striking pink-red veins. It wants warmth, high humidity and a rich, well-drained mix. Water when the surface dries and keep it shaded from direct sun to protect the dark foliage.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring.

directions: This upright species has long, glossy dark green leaves and handles low light better than most houseplants. Grow it in a peaty mix and water when the top inch dries. Keep it warm and wipe the broad leaves occasionally to keep their shine.

planting.note: Repot and divide in spring.

directions: Distinctive for its long, narrow blade-shaped leaves marbled in silvery green. Grow it in a well-drained houseplant mix with bright, indirect light and let the top inch dry between waterings. Its slim upright form suits tight spaces and tabletops.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring.

directions: A small, low-growing species with dark green leaves marked by a pale central band along the midrib. It prefers warm, humid, shaded conditions and a rich, well-drained mix. Keep it evenly moist and divide the spreading clump to propagate.

planting.note: Root cane cuttings in spring warmth.

directions: This popular cultivar has creamy yellow leaf centers bordered by a thin green margin. Grow it in a rich, well-drained mix in bright, indirect light and water when the top inch dries. Wear gloves when taking cuttings, as the sap is irritating.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring.

directions: Named for its soft chartreuse leaves overlaid with a pale mottled pattern, this cultivar brightens shaded corners. Use a well-drained mix and water when the surface begins to dry. Keep it warm and out of direct sun, which bleaches the delicate coloring.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring warmth.

directions: This striking cultivar shows dark green leaves dappled with lime and cream flecks around a pale midrib. Grow it in a rich, freely draining mix with bright, indirect light and steady humidity. Water when the top inch dries and keep it away from cold windows.

planting.note: Root cane cuttings in spring.

directions: This species has solid deep green leaves with a distinct pale white midrib rather than heavy variegation. Grow it in a well-drained mix and water when the top inch dries. Give it bright, indirect light and warm, humid air for steady growth.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring warmth.

directions: Grown for arrow-shaped leaves that are deep green above and rich burgundy below, this vine climbs or trails readily. Use a light, well-drained mix and keep it evenly moist with bright, indirect light. Pinch the tips to keep it bushy or provide a pole for climbing.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring.

directions: This compact species has near-black velvety leaves split by a bright silver-white central vein. Grow it in an airy, well-drained mix with high humidity and bright, indirect light. Keep it lightly moist and let it climb a small support to encourage larger leaves.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring warmth.

directions: This bold species has large, thick blue-green leaves with a subtle silvery sheen. Grow it in a chunky, well-drained mix and provide a pole so the leaves can reach full size. Water when the top inch dries and keep it in warm, bright, indirect light.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring.

directions: A vigorous species with large, matte green arrowhead leaves on a climbing stem. Grow it in a well-drained aroid mix and give it a moss pole to climb for the biggest foliage. Keep it evenly moist in bright, indirect light and warm conditions.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring warmth.

directions: This compact cultivar has pale green arrowhead leaves veined and blushed in soft pink. Give it bright, indirect light to bring out the pink tones and water when the top inch dries. Pinch it regularly to keep the mounding, bushy habit.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring.

directions: A widely grown cultivar with bubblegum-pink arrowhead leaves that mature to a softer green-pink. Grow it in a light, well-drained mix and keep it in bright, indirect light for the strongest color. Water when the top inch dries and pinch to maintain a full shape.

planting.note: Root cuttings any time in the warm season.

directions: This heavily variegated pothos has leaves marbled in creamy white and green and trails or climbs easily. Give it bright, indirect light to keep the variegation strong and water when the top inch or two dries. Root stem cuttings in water or moist mix to propagate.

planting.note: Root cuttings during the warm months.

directions: This cultivar has solid chartreuse leaves that glow brightest in good indirect light. Grow it in any well-drained houseplant mix and water when the top inch or two dries. Trim long vines and re-root the cuttings to keep the plant full.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm season.

directions: A compact cultivar with small leaves sharply divided into green centers and crisp white margins. Give it bright, indirect light to hold the variegation and water when the top inch dries. Its shorter internodes make a tidy, bushy trailing plant.

planting.note: Root cuttings during warm weather.

directions: This small-leaved cultivar has green and white variegation flecked with fine gray-green speckling toward the margins. It grows a bit slower than common pothos, so keep it in bright, indirect light. Water when the top inch dries and propagate from stem cuttings.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm season.

directions: This patented cultivar has wavy, heart-shaped leaves splashed with cream, white and green in irregular patches. Grow it in bright, indirect light to keep the varied coloring and water when the top inch or two dries. Root cuttings to propagate and keep the plant bushy.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm months.

directions: This newer cultivar shows deep green leaves marbled with lighter mossy green centers. Grow it in bright, indirect light and a well-drained mix, watering when the top inch dries. It roots easily from stem cuttings placed in water or moist soil.

planting.note: Root cuttings any time in warm weather.

directions: The solid green cultivar of pothos, this is among the toughest and most shade-tolerant trailing houseplants. Grow it in any well-drained mix and water when the top inch or two dries. It roots quickly from stem cuttings, making it easy to multiply.

planting.note: Root cuttings during the warm season.

directions: This cultivar is even more heavily white-variegated than Marble Queen, with leaves that are mostly cream flecked with green. It needs bright, indirect light to fuel the sparse green tissue and grows slowly. Water when the top inch dries and avoid direct sun on the pale leaves.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm season.

directions: This cultivar has larger leaves than the species, heavily brushed with big silver blotches over deep green. Grow it in a well-drained mix and let the top inch dry before watering, as it dislikes soggy roots. Give it bright, indirect light and let it trail or climb a small pole.

planting.note: Root cuttings during warm weather.

directions: This compact cultivar has small dark green leaves edged and dotted with neat silver markings. Grow it in a well-drained mix and water when the top inch dries, since it is sensitive to overwatering. Bright, indirect light keeps the leaves small and richly marked.

planting.note: Set out corms or potted divisions only once nights stay above 55 F and soil is warm.

directions: Plant the corm shoulder just below the surface in a chunky, fast-draining aroid mix and water when the top inch dries. Prize the arrowhead leaves for their glossy dark faces, silvery veins, and pink-blushed stems, which color best in bright indirect light. In frost-free ground it forms a small clump; elsewhere overwinter the corm dry and dormant.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors after the last frost when the soil has thoroughly warmed.

directions: Grow this upright species in a deep pot of loose, humus-rich mix, keeping the crown at soil level. The long ruffled leaves are olive-green above with striking purple-red undersides that show off when backlit. Feed lightly through the warm season and let the surface dry slightly between waterings to avoid rot.

planting.note: Plant out or repot in spring once temperatures are reliably above 55 F.

directions: This vigorous zebrina hybrid wants a large container of rich, well-drained mix and bright filtered light. Set the corm shallowly and support the tall stems, which carry big arrow leaves with bold white veins over patterned green stalks. Water when the top few inches dry and shelter it from strong wind that can tear the foliage.

planting.note: Best grown as a warm container plant; move outside only in settled warm weather.

directions: Pot this quirky dwarf hybrid in a small, snug container of airy aroid mix with the corm just covered. Its cupped, teardrop leaves ride on thin whimsical stems that twist toward the light, so give it bright indirect exposure and turn it regularly. Keep humidity up and water only after the surface has dried to prevent stem rot.

planting.note: A tender dwarf best kept in a pot and moved out only during warm settled weather.

directions: This miniature sanderiana-type stays small, so a compact pot of gritty, well-drained mix suits it well. Narrow, deeply lobed dark leaves with silvery veins and purple backs stand upright on slender stems. Provide warmth, steady humidity, and bright indirect light, watering only when the top inch feels dry.

planting.note: Grow warm year-round; place outdoors only after frost danger passes.

directions: This slow, compact jewel hybrid has thick, stiff leaves in dusty grey-green with a heavily quilted, dragon-scale texture. Use a very open aroid mix, keep the corm shallow, and water sparingly since it resents soggy roots. Bright indirect light and warm, humid air keep it in active leaf.

planting.note: Set out or repot in spring once nights hold above 55 F.

directions: This Philippine species carries broad, shield-shaped leaves in apple-green with darker green veining and thick, slightly wavy margins. Plant in loose, quick-draining mix with the crown at the surface and give bright filtered light. Allow the top of the soil to dry between waterings and keep it warm and humid for steady new leaves.

planting.note: A tender collector jewel; keep warm and grow outdoors only in hot, settled weather.

directions: This Borneo species has thick, stiff, dusty grey-green leaves with a deeply corrugated, almost reptilian surface. It grows in mineral, sharply drained media over its native ultramafic soils, so use a gritty mix and water carefully. Give warmth, high humidity, and bright indirect light, letting the surface dry between drinks.

planting.note: Strictly tender; grow as a warm indoor or greenhouse plant most of the year.

directions: A small, slow jewel with nearly black, glossy leaves flushed iridescent crimson-purple and edged by contrasting pale veins. Pot it in an airy, well-drained aroid mix and keep the corm shallow. It demands consistent warmth and high humidity, bright indirect light, and only enough water to keep the mix lightly moist.

planting.note: A tender jewel species; keep it warm and never expose it to frost.

directions: This Borneo jewel bears thick, silvery blue-grey leaves with dark green veining and a subtle metallic sheen. Grow it lean in a gritty, fast-draining mineral mix, keeping the corm just at the surface. Steady warmth, high humidity, and bright filtered light suit it, and let the soil approach dry before watering.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost in warm soil; overwinter the corm where frost occurs.

directions: This bold species forms a large upright clump of broad leaves shaded metallic purple-grey with dark stems. Plant in deep, rich, moisture-retentive soil and water generously through the growing season. Give it warm filtered sun and shelter from wind; in cold climates lift and store the corm dry over winter.

planting.note: Set out in warm soil after frost; provide shade from harsh midday sun to protect pale leaf zones.

directions: This striking sport carries huge upright leaves irregularly marbled and blotched in cream and green, with every leaf patterned differently. Plant the corm in deep, fertile, evenly moist soil and feed through the warm months for size. Give bright filtered light rather than scorching sun, and lift the corm to store frost-free in cold regions.

planting.note: Move outdoors only in settled warm weather; keep frost off the corm.

directions: This hybrid offers narrow, arrow-shaped green leaves with pale ivory veins carried on pink-flushed stems. Plant the corm shallowly in loose, well-drained aroid mix and site it in bright indirect light. Keep it warm and humid, watering when the top inch dries and easing off as growth slows in cool weather.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in warm soil; overwinter the corm indoors where it freezes.

directions: A tall, tough hybrid with glossy, near-black upright leaves on dark stems that make a dramatic vertical accent. Plant in rich, well-drained soil and give bright light, which deepens the dark coloring. Water when the surface dries and feed through summer; lift the corm to store dry over winter in cold zones.

planting.note: A tender collector species; keep warm and never expose to frost.

directions: This narrow-leaved Philippine species has stiff, leathery, lance-shaped blades in deep green with paler sunken veins. Grow it in a gritty, sharply drained aroid mix with the corm at the surface. Provide warmth, high humidity, and bright filtered light, watering only when the top of the mix has dried.

planting.note: Strictly tender; keep warm and grow under glass or indoors in cool climates.

directions: This small Borneo jewel has very dark, near-black leaves that flash metallic blue-purple in good light. Pot it in an airy, fast-draining mineral mix and keep the corm shallow. Constant warmth and high humidity are essential, and water sparingly so the roots never sit wet.

planting.note: Plant tubers outdoors once soil is warm after the last frost.

directions: This green elephant ear develops a bold creamy-yellow blaze that spreads from the center of each mature leaf as the season warms. Plant tubers a few inches deep in rich, constantly moist soil and feed heavily for big leaves. Give full sun to light shade with abundant water, or grow it at a pond margin; lift tubers before frost in cold zones.

planting.note: Plant out in warm soil after frost; a compact selection good for containers.

directions: This dwarf elephant ear pairs deep green leaves with vivid red-pink stems and matching red veins for a compact splash of color. Plant the tuber in rich, moisture-retentive soil and never let it dry out during active growth. It thrives in sun to part shade with steady water and makes an excellent pot or pond-edge plant.

planting.note: Set out tubers when soil warms after the last frost.

directions: This selection has narrow, rippled black-purple leaves with a puckered surface and a distinctly ruffled edge. Plant tubers in fertile, boggy soil and keep them wet through the growing season for lush growth. Full sun deepens the dark color; feed generously and lift tubers to overwinter where the ground freezes.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when soil is warm; overwinter tubers indoors in cold climates.

directions: This robust hybrid carries large dark leaves with a smoky purple cast and bold contrasting veins on sturdy stems. Plant the tuber in rich, constantly moist soil and feed heavily for maximum leaf size. It takes full sun with ample water and shrugs off heat; lift and store the tuber dry before hard frost.

planting.note: Plant tubers in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: New leaves emerge coffee-brown and mature to deep coffee-green over dark chocolate-colored stems for a warm, moody effect. Plant the tuber in rich, wet soil and keep it soaked all season, ideally at a pond edge or in a large pot standing in a saucer. Full sun and heavy feeding drive its best size and color.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in warm, wet soil.

directions: This eye-catching selection has green leaves splashed with cream and pink around bright red-pink veins and stems. Plant tubers in fertile, boggy soil and keep them constantly wet for lush, colorful foliage. Give full sun to part shade with heavy feeding, and lift the tuber to store dry over winter in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant tubers in warm soil after frost; a compact color selection.

directions: This bright selection glows chartreuse to lime-yellow with darker veining and a small dark center marking. Plant the tuber in rich, wet soil and keep it evenly moist for the most vivid color. Give it dappled to part sun, since strong midday exposure can bleach the pale leaves, and feed regularly through summer.

planting.note: Set out in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: This dramatic hybrid shows a creamy-white central river flowing down the midrib of each dark green leaf. Plant tubers in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and keep them wet through the season for the boldest markings. It grows well in full sun to part shade with heavy feeding; overwinter the tuber indoors where it freezes.

planting.note: Plant out in warm soil after frost; needs a long, hot season for full size.

directions: This towering green selection produces enormous ribbed leaves on tall, sturdy stems for a true tropical statement. Plant the tuber in deep, rich, constantly moist soil and feed heavily all season. Give it full sun with abundant water; in cold climates dig and store the large tuber dry over winter.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; roots survive winter in the ground in milder zones.

directions: This spreading dwarf species has small, rounded matte green leaves with a silvery central sheen and runs gently by underground stems. Plant in rich, moist to wet soil in part shade and keep it well watered. Hardier than most elephant ears, it can be left in the ground with a mulch in mild-winter areas.

planting.note: A small tender species; grow warm in a pot and move out only in warm weather.

directions: This compact species has small, quilted leaves boldly marked with dark purple-black between silvery green veins. Grow it in rich, evenly moist soil in bright indirect light or dappled shade, keeping humidity high. Its modest size makes it ideal for containers, terrariums, and shaded pond edges.

planting.note: Plant out in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: This hybrid displays large dark leaves marbled and streaked with lighter green and cream in a shifting pattern. Plant tubers in rich, constantly moist soil and feed heavily for the fullest leaves. It performs in full sun to part shade with plenty of water; lift the tuber to overwinter dry in cold zones.

planting.note: Set out tubers in warm soil after frost has passed.

directions: This bold selection carries deep blackish-purple leaves on nearly black stems for a strong dark accent. Plant tubers in fertile, wet soil and keep them soaked through the growing season. Full sun intensifies the dark coloring; feed generously and store the tuber dry indoors before hard frost.

planting.note: Plant out in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: This distinctive hybrid has deeply quilted, upward-cupped leaves with dark purple-green faces and boldly sunken chartreuse veins. Plant the tuber in rich, constantly moist soil and keep it wet through the warm months. It takes full sun to part shade with heavy feeding, and its tuber should be lifted and stored dry over winter in cold climates.

planting.note: Plant tubers only once the soil is thoroughly warm, well after the last frost.

directions: This strap-leaf caladium bears slender, mostly white lance-shaped leaves with fine green edges and veins, tolerating more sun than many. Plant tubers knobby-side up a couple of inches deep in warm, moist, well-drained soil. Keep evenly watered and warm; foliage colors up within a few weeks and fades back as cool weather returns.

planting.note: Wait for warm soil before planting tubers, well after frost.

directions: This compact strap-leaf caladium has narrow, ruffled red leaves edged in green for a lacy, colorful mound. Plant tubers a couple of inches deep in warm, moist, humus-rich soil and keep them evenly watered. Its dwarf habit suits pots and edging in dappled sun to part shade; the tuber goes dormant as temperatures drop.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm soil only, well after the last frost.

directions: This fancy-leaf caladium shows vivid scarlet-red centers bordered by deep green with contrasting red veining. Plant tubers a couple of inches deep in warm, moist, well-drained soil and keep humidity up. Grow in filtered light to part shade for the richest color, watering steadily until the foliage naturally dies back in fall.

planting.note: Plant tubers once soil is warm, well after frost.

directions: This strap-leaf caladium pairs white leaves with bold red central veins and a thin green margin, and it tolerates considerable sun. Plant tubers a couple of inches deep in warm, moist, well-drained soil. Water evenly and keep it warm; the crisp white-and-red foliage holds well until cool weather sends the tuber dormant.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm soil, well after the last frost.

directions: This fancy-leaf caladium has heart-shaped leaves washed rosy-red at the center over a green ground with red veining. Plant tubers a couple of inches deep in warm, moist, humus-rich soil in shade to part shade. Keep the soil evenly moist and the air humid; the leaves reliably return each summer from the overwintered tuber.

planting.note: Plant tubers once soil warms well after frost.

directions: This fancy-leaf caladium has luminous near-white leaves netted with fine green veins for a cool, bright look in shade. Plant tubers a couple of inches deep in warm, moist, well-drained soil and keep them evenly watered. Grow in shade to filtered light to protect the pale leaves, and lift the tuber to store dry over winter in cold zones.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm soil, well after the last frost.

directions: This colorful caladium shows white leaves peppered with cranberry-red flecks and spots over green veins. Plant tubers a couple of inches deep in warm, moist, humus-rich soil and keep them evenly watered. Filtered light to part shade brings out the speckling; the foliage returns each warm season from the stored tuber.

planting.note: Plant tubers once the soil is warm, well after frost.

directions: This large fancy-leaf caladium has creamy-white leaves flushed strawberry-pink at the center and dusted with red speckles. Plant tubers a couple of inches deep in warm, moist, well-drained soil rich in organic matter. Give filtered light to part shade and steady moisture; the bold leaves hold until cool weather sends the tuber dormant.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm soil, well after the last frost.

directions: This caladium pairs a soft white-to-pink field with a green border and raspberry-red central blotches and speckles. Plant tubers a couple of inches deep in warm, moist, humus-rich soil and keep them evenly watered. Filtered light to part shade suits it best; overwinter the dormant tuber dry and warm where frost occurs.

planting.note: Root cuttings indoors any time of year; spring gives the fastest new growth.

directions: Take a four to six inch stem cutting with at least two nodes and set it in water or moist potting mix until roots form. Grow in bright indirect light and let the top inch of soil dry between waterings. The heart-shaped leaves streaked with gold signal healthy, vigorous growth; trim long vines to keep the plant full.

planting.note: Cuttings root indoors year round; provide a support to encourage larger mature leaves.

directions: Root a nodal cutting in water or mix, then pot in a chunky, well-draining medium. Grown on a moss pole in bright light, mature leaves may fenestrate and take on a silvery blue sheen. Water when the top inch dries and avoid letting it sit wet.

planting.note: Root cuttings indoors; enjoys warmth and moderate humidity.

directions: Take a cutting below a node and root it in water or moist mix before potting. The matte green leaves are dusted with silver spots that show best in bright indirect light. Let the top inch of soil dry between waterings, as this plant dislikes constantly wet roots.

planting.note: Divide the rhizome in spring or summer for the strongest recovery.

directions: Divide a clump so each piece keeps roots and a few leaves, then pot in a fast-draining cactus mix. The upright leaves carry bright yellow margins that stay truest when divided rather than leaf-propagated. Water sparingly, letting the soil dry fully, and it tolerates a wide range of light.

planting.note: Separate offsets in the warm season and pot them shallowly.

directions: Lift and separate pups from the parent rosette, keeping some roots on each. This dwarf form makes a tight funnel of leaves that stays under a foot tall, ideal for desks and shelves. Use gritty, well-draining soil and water only when the mix is thoroughly dry.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes during active growth to keep the pale color true.

directions: Divide a rhizome clump and pot each section in a fast-draining mix. The broad, upright leaves are a silvery pale green that deepens in lower light, so give it bright indirect light to keep the moonlit tone. Water infrequently and let the soil dry completely between drinks.

planting.note: Split the clump in spring or summer for quick establishment.

directions: Separate a rooted section from the parent and pot in gritty, well-draining soil. Its dark green leaves are banded with silvery green horizontal markings and hold an upright habit. Water only when fully dry and place in low to bright indirect light, avoiding cold drafts.

planting.note: Divide offsets in the warm months and let cut surfaces callus before potting.

directions: Separate pups from the base and pot in a sandy, fast-draining mix once any cuts have dried. The round, spear-like leaves grow stiffly upright and are sometimes braided while young. Water sparingly, allowing the soil to dry out fully, and give bright indirect light.

planting.note: Divide the rhizome in spring; this slow grower resents frequent disturbance.

directions: Divide the thick rhizome so each piece carries a fan-shaped leaf and roots, then pot in gritty mix. The single broad, paddle-like leaves resemble a whale's fin and can grow very large over time. Water only when the soil is bone dry and keep it out of cold, wet conditions.

planting.note: Separate pups in warm months and pot after cut ends have callused.

directions: Divide the clump so each fan of leaves keeps roots, then pot in a fast-draining succulent mix. The short, fat cylindrical leaves splay outward like the arms of a starfish. Water only when fully dry and give bright indirect light to keep the compact form.

planting.note: Divide offsets rather than leaf-propagate to keep the yellow margins.

directions: Split rooted offsets from the parent rosette and pot them shallowly in gritty mix. This dwarf bird's nest form has wide yellow leaf edges surrounding a green center. Water only when the soil dries completely and give bright indirect light to hold the variegation.

planting.note: Divide clumps in the warm season for reliable establishment.

directions: Separate rooted divisions and pot in a well-draining succulent blend. The short leaves twist and curl as they grow and carry golden margins with a green center. Water sparingly once the mix is fully dry and place in bright indirect light.

planting.note: Divide the rhizome in spring or summer for quick recovery.

directions: Divide a rooted section and pot in fast-draining soil. This compact selection has shorter, wider leaves than Laurentii with yellow margins that stay bright in good light. Let the soil dry fully between waterings and avoid leaving water in the leaf funnel.

planting.note: Pot up plantlets or divide clumps any time indoors, best in spring.

directions: Root a runner plantlet in water or soil, or divide an established clump, and pot in general houseplant mix. This form has a central white or cream stripe running down each arching green leaf. Keep the soil lightly moist and use filtered or distilled water to prevent brown leaf tips.

planting.note: Propagate from plantlets or divide the crown, ideally in spring.

directions: Pot a rooted offshoot or divide a mature plant into a well-draining mix. Unlike Vittatum, this cultivar has green centers with white or cream leaf margins. Keep the soil evenly moist, provide bright indirect light, and rinse the roots periodically to reduce tip burn.

planting.note: Root plantlets or divide clumps indoors, spring for best growth.

directions: Detach a runner plantlet and root it, or divide an existing clump, then pot in a light mix. This compact form has the same cream-striped leaves as Vittatum but they curl and kink attractively. Keep lightly moist and give bright indirect light to encourage the curling and plentiful runners.

planting.note: Divide clumps or root runners any time, spring preferred.

directions: Root a plantlet from a runner or divide a mature clump and pot in ordinary houseplant soil. The all-green species tolerates lower light better than the variegated forms and grows vigorously. Keep the soil lightly moist and let it send out arching stems of small white flowers followed by baby plantlets.

planting.note: Divide the crown in spring; unlike common spider plants it rarely makes runners.

directions: Divide an established rosette at the crown since this relative does not produce trailing plantlets. It forms a clump of broad green leaves with striking coral-orange leaf stalks and midribs. Keep evenly moist in bright indirect light and remove faded leaves to show off the orange stems.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring; new growth emerges green and darkens with age.

directions: Divide the potato-like rhizomes so each piece has a growth point and roots, then pot in a well-draining mix. New stems unfurl bright lime green and mature to a near-black purple in good light. Water only when the soil is dry, as the thick rhizomes store moisture and rot easily if overwatered.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in the warm season; this dwarf form grows slowly.

directions: Separate rhizome sections with roots and pot in a gritty, fast-draining mix. This compact cultivar has shorter, denser stems with tightly stacked dark green leaflets. Water sparingly only after the soil dries out, and it thrives in low to bright indirect light.

planting.note: Divide the crown in spring so each piece keeps several leaves and roots.

directions: Divide a mature clump into sections that each carry roots and foliage, then pot in a rich, moisture-retentive mix. This compact species peace lily blooms readily with white hooded spathes in low to moderate light. Keep the soil evenly moist and the drooping leaves are a clear signal it needs water.

planting.note: Divide the compact crown in spring for the best flowering the same year.

directions: Split the small clump into rooted pieces and pot in a peat-based mix that holds moisture. This dwarf cultivar stays under a foot tall yet flowers freely with small white spathes. Keep the soil consistently moist and give low to moderate indirect light, watering promptly if leaves begin to wilt.

planting.note: Root stem or cane cuttings in the warm season for reliable rooting.

directions: Root a tip or cane cutting in moist mix and pot once roots develop. The sword-shaped leaves have bright chartreuse margins framing a gray-green center. Keep lightly moist with filtered or distilled water to avoid leaf-tip burn, and give bright indirect light.

planting.note: Propagate from stem cuttings during active warm-season growth.

directions: Root a cane or tip cutting in a moist, well-draining mix before potting up. The broad leaves emerge a vivid solid lime green, brightest in good indirect light. Water when the top inch dries, use low-fluoride water to prevent tip browning, and keep away from cold drafts.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in the warm season for best success.

directions: Take a stem cutting and root it in moist mix, then pot once established. The narrow, whorled leaves carry lighter green stripes, a subtler pattern than the yellow-edged Song of India. Water when the top inch of soil dries and give bright indirect light for compact, bushy growth.

planting.note: Root stem or cane cuttings in warmth; this dwarf grows very slowly.

directions: Root a short cane cutting in moist mix and be patient, as this compact form is slow to establish. It bears tight rosettes of short, dark green leaves clustered along stubby stems. Water when the top inch dries, use filtered water to prevent tip burn, and keep in bright indirect light.

planting.note: Repot or divide in spring once new growth resumes and nights stay warm.

directions: Pot divisions at the same depth they grew, in a peaty, fast-draining mix, and keep the crown just above the soil line. This plant wants steady moisture, warmth, and high humidity, so mist or use a pebble tray and never let it dry fully. Watch for the large silver-and-green banded leaves to unfurl as the sign a division has established.

planting.note: Divide and pot in spring so the plant can build strength before its flowering push.

directions: Set the division into a rich, well-drained mix with the growing point level with the surface, then water in gently. Unlike most in this group it is grown for its blooms, so give bright indirect light and consistent moisture to encourage flowering. Expect the upright orange bracts to appear, which is your cue the plant is thriving.

planting.note: Repot in spring as temperatures climb and root activity picks up.

directions: Plant divisions into a loose, humus-rich mix, keeping the crown at soil level and firming lightly around the roots. The velvety two-toned leaves burn in direct sun and brown at the edges in dry air, so favor filtered light and high humidity. Cream, cone-shaped flower spikes may appear on healthy, mature clumps.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and pot immediately to avoid root desiccation.

directions: Settle divisions into a peaty, well-draining mix with the crown at surface level and water thoroughly. The wavy, fuzzy-backed leaves respond to steady moisture and warm, humid air, so keep the mix lightly damp and away from cold drafts. New spear-like leaves unrolling with their reddish undersides show the division has taken.

planting.note: Repot or split in spring while the plant is actively pushing new leaves.

directions: Plant the division at its original depth in a light, moisture-retentive mix and press gently to seat the roots. This one tolerates slightly more forgiving conditions than most prayer plants but still wants bright indirect light and even moisture. The fine mosaic-patterned foliage staying crisp and green tells you the humidity and watering are right.

planting.note: Divide gently in spring, keeping several leaves per section for a fast start.

directions: Set the division into a peaty, airy mix at soil level and water in with room-temperature water. The variegated white sectors make this cultivar especially thirsty and humidity-sensitive, so maintain consistent moisture and 60 percent or higher humidity. Fresh leaves opening with clean white-and-green marbling confirm it has settled in.

planting.note: Repot in spring and divide crowded clumps at the same time.

directions: Pot the division level with the surface in a rich, free-draining mix and keep it evenly moist. Its slender, zebra-striped leaves brown quickly in dry air or hard water, so use filtered or rainwater and maintain good humidity. Steady production of new upright leaves signals a well-established plant.

planting.note: Split and repot in spring while the plant is in active growth.

directions: Settle the division into a moisture-retentive but airy mix, keeping the crown at soil height. The broad silver leaves edged in green need bright indirect light and consistently damp soil to avoid curling. Compact new leaves emerging from the center show the division has rooted well.

planting.note: Repot in spring and divide only well-rooted, multi-stemmed clumps.

directions: Plant the division at its former depth in a peaty, well-drained mix and water until it drains freely. The pale-centered leaves with dark green margins scorch in direct sun and crisp in dry air, so give filtered light and steady humidity. Look for new rounded leaves with the signature light halo as proof it is thriving.

planting.note: Divide in spring, keeping a healthy fan of leaves on each piece.

directions: Set the division level with the soil in a rich, quick-draining mix and keep it evenly moist. The rose-pink leaf centers with deep green edges fade if light is too strong, so bright indirect light suits it best. New leaves unfurling with vivid pink centers indicate the plant is well settled.

planting.note: Repot or divide in spring as growth accelerates.

directions: Pot the division at its original depth in an airy, moisture-holding mix and water thoroughly. The dark leaves with feathered pale-green blotches and purple undersides need filtered light and reliably damp soil. Consistent new leaf production from the crown shows a well-established division.

planting.note: Divide crowded clumps in spring and repot into fresh mix.

directions: Plant the division level with the surface in a peaty, well-draining mix and water in gently. The narrow leaves with fine pink and silver pinstriping brown at the tips in dry or chlorinated conditions, so use filtered water and keep humidity high. Fresh striped leaves emerging upright confirm the plant has established.

planting.note: Repot in spring and divide well-rooted clumps at the same time.

directions: Set the division at soil level in a rich, free-draining mix and keep it consistently moist. The broad leaves with feathery green-and-cream markings and purple backs prefer bright indirect light and humid air. Steady unfurling of patterned new leaves shows the plant is happy.

planting.note: Divide and repot in spring as new shoots appear.

directions: Pot the division at its original depth in a light, well-draining mix and water in. The lance-shaped leaves marked with dark brushstroke patterns are somewhat more tolerant than fussier calatheas but still want even moisture and indirect light. Compact new leaves from the base indicate a rooted, settled plant.

planting.note: Repot or split in spring during active growth.

directions: Plant the division level with the mix surface in a peaty, moisture-retentive blend and water thoroughly. The crisp green leaves with thin white feathered stripes need bright filtered light and steady humidity to keep their edges from browning. Regular emergence of freshly striped leaves shows the division has established.

planting.note: Divide in spring, keeping each section well rooted before potting.

directions: Set the division at soil level in a rich, airy mix and keep it evenly moist. This compact species has rippled leaves with a silver central band and purple undersides, and it is especially sensitive to dry air and hard water. Small new wavy leaves opening from the crown confirm it is established.

planting.note: Repot in spring and divide only vigorous, established clumps.

directions: Pot the division level with the surface in a moisture-retentive, well-draining mix and water in well. The large rounded leaves with fine dark green banding need bright indirect light and consistently humid air. Broad new leaves unfurling from the center show the plant has settled successfully.

planting.note: Divide clumps or root stem cuttings in spring for quickest establishment.

directions: Lay the division or cutting shallowly in a peaty, well-draining mix with the roots just covered, and keep the soil evenly damp. This low, spreading plant shows dark leaves with bright red veins and folds them upward at night, so give it indirect light and steady humidity. New trailing shoots with vivid red venation confirm it has rooted.

planting.note: Divide or take cuttings in spring while the plant is actively growing.

directions: Set the division shallowly in a light, moisture-retentive mix and firm gently around the roots. Known as Rabbit's Tracks for the dark blotches flanking its midrib, this trailing plant likes indirect light, even moisture, and warm air. Fresh runners spreading over the pot edge indicate it has established.

planting.note: Root cuttings or divide in spring for a fast start.

directions: Plant the division just below the surface in a peaty, free-draining mix and keep it evenly moist. This cultivar carries the same herringbone pattern in bright chartreuse and yellow-green tones and wants indirect light with high humidity. Spreading new shoots in vivid lime color show it has rooted well.

planting.note: Divide in spring and pot immediately, keeping the roots moist.

directions: Settle the division shallowly into a moisture-retentive, well-draining mix and water in gently. This form has dark, near-black leaves with silvery veins and a green feathered midrib, thriving in indirect light and humid, warm conditions. New trailing growth with its silver-veined leaves confirms establishment.

planting.note: Divide well-rooted clumps in spring and repot into fresh mix.

directions: Plant the division level with the surface in a rich, well-draining mix and water thoroughly. The cream, green, and pink variegated leaves with wine-red undersides need bright indirect light and steady humidity to hold their color and avoid crisping. New leaves opening with fresh tricolor variegation show the plant has established.

planting.note: Repot or divide in spring during active growth.

directions: Set the division at soil level in a peaty, free-draining mix and keep it consistently moist. This compact selection carries finely speckled cream-and-green variegation over reddish undersides and wants bright filtered light with humid air. Freshly speckled new leaves emerging from the crown indicate the plant is settled.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring and repot promptly.

directions: Pot the division level with the surface in a rich, well-draining mix and water in well. Also called Golden Mosaic, its green leaves are splashed with yellow variegation and it tolerates slightly more light than fussier relatives while still wanting even moisture. Tall new shoots with fresh gold marbling show the division has established.

planting.note: Repot or split in spring while the plant is in active growth.

directions: Set the division at soil level in an airy, moisture-retentive mix and water thoroughly. The large silvery-grey leaves with fine green banding and burgundy undersides want bright indirect light and steady humidity. Upright new leaves unfurling from long petioles confirm the plant has established.

planting.note: Pot up rooted cuttings in spring as light levels rise and new growth resumes.

directions: Root a stem-tip cutting in moist medium and set the young plant in a pot of free-draining houseplant mix. Give it bright, indirect light so the cream-and-green variegation stays crisp, and let the top inch of soil dry between waterings. Wipe the broad leaves occasionally and the plant will push out fresh pink-sheathed growth through the warm months.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings in spring so the plant establishes ahead of the growing season.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in a snug pot of loose, well-draining mix and place it where it receives plenty of bright, filtered light to bring out the pink and rose tones. Water thoroughly, then wait until the surface soil dries before watering again. The brightest new leaves appear on plants kept warm and out of cold drafts.

planting.note: Move rooted cuttings into their pots in spring for the strongest first flush.

directions: Set a rooted cutting into a container of gritty, well-draining potting soil and grow it in bright indirect light to deepen the near-black burgundy leaf color. Allow the top of the soil to dry before each watering and avoid soggy roots. Stake the single stem if it leans, and pinch the tip to encourage branching once it reaches the desired height.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring as the days lengthen.

directions: Transplant a rooted cutting into a pot of open, free-draining mix and give it a bright spot out of direct midday sun. This selection carries especially large, thick, glossy green leaves, so wipe them clean now and then to keep them dust-free. Water when the top inch dries and feed lightly during spring and summer to fuel the broad new foliage.

planting.note: Establish rooted cuttings in spring so they bush out over summer.

directions: Pot a rooted cutting of this compact, bushy selection in well-draining houseplant mix and place it in bright, indirect light. Its smaller, densely set dark leaves make it a tidy tabletop plant, so keep it evenly moist but never waterlogged. Turn the pot every week or two so growth stays balanced on all sides.

planting.note: Pot up rooted cuttings in spring and keep them in a stable, draft-free spot.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in free-draining mix and give this white-and-green weeping fig the brightest indirect light you can offer to hold its variegation. Keep it in a steady position, since sudden moves or cold drafts trigger leaf drop. Water when the top inch of soil dries and let the arching, willowy branches cascade for a graceful indoor tree.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings into pots in spring for the best establishment.

directions: Grow a rooted cutting in a pot of loose, well-draining soil and place it in bright, filtered light away from heating vents. This selection holds dark, uniformly green leaves and forms a full, upright canopy over time. Water when the surface dries, and expect some leaf shedding whenever the plant is relocated until it re-settles.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Set a rooted cutting of this dwarf, small-leaved fiddle-leaf into a pot of well-draining mix and place it in consistent bright, indirect light. Its compact habit suits a shelf or tabletop, but it still resents overwatering, so let the top couple of inches dry first. Keep it in one spot and away from drafts to avoid dropped leaves.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings in spring for a strong season of growth.

directions: Pot a rooted cutting in free-draining houseplant mix and grow it in bright, indirect light for the fullest, most closely spaced leaves. This selection stays denser and shorter than the standard species, making a bushy indoor specimen. Water thoroughly when the top of the soil dries and dust the large leaves so they photosynthesize well.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring for best establishment.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in a pot of well-draining mix and give this small-leaved fig bright, indirect light. Its distinctive wedge-shaped green leaves stay glossy when the plant is watered once the top inch dries and kept away from cold. In good light it may set tiny ornamental figs along the stems.

planting.note: Establish rooted cuttings in spring in bright light to hold variegation.

directions: Set a rooted cutting into free-draining potting mix and place it in bright, indirect light so the creamy leaf margins stay well defined. Let the top inch of soil dry between waterings, as the variegated tissue is less tolerant of soggy roots. Turn the plant regularly and keep it warm to encourage even, colorful new growth.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring so they settle before summer growth.

directions: Grow a rooted cutting in a pot of well-draining mix and give this long-leaved fig bright, indirect light in a stable location. Its narrow, willow-like foliage makes it far less prone to leaf drop than the weeping fig, though it still dislikes drafts. Water when the top inch of soil dries and prune lightly to shape the upright form.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring to establish through the warm season.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in free-draining houseplant mix and place this small, shrubby fig in bright, indirect light. It readily produces pea-sized yellow-green fruit even as a young indoor plant, which is its main ornamental charm. Water when the surface soil dries and keep it warm and humid for steady fruiting.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring in bright light for the strongest color.

directions: Set a rooted cutting into a pot of open, well-draining mix and place this bold, broad-leaved fig in bright, indirect light to bring out its yellow-green variegation. It grows into a substantial upright indoor tree, so give it room and turn it for even growth. Water when the top inch dries and wipe the large leaves clean periodically.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring so they establish over the growing season.

directions: Grow a rooted cutting in a pot of free-draining mix and place this soft-leaved banyan in bright, indirect light. Its velvety, pale-veined leaves are more forgiving than a fiddle-leaf fig, but it still wants consistent care and warmth. Water when the top inch of soil dries and prune to keep the upright tree form full.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring; can summer outdoors in warm regions.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in well-draining mix and give this Australian fig bright light, tolerating some direct sun once acclimated. The undersides of its leaves carry a rusty felt that gives the plant its name, and it makes a durable container or bonsai subject. Water when the top inch dries and prune freely to control its vigorous size.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring for reliable establishment.

directions: Set a rooted cutting into free-draining potting mix and place this broad, long-leaved fig in bright, indirect light. Its wide, drooping leaves give a lush look while resisting the leaf drop common to weeping figs. Water when the top inch of soil dries and keep it in a stable, warm spot for steady growth.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and pot in a small hanging container.

directions: Lay a trailing cutting on the surface of a well-draining, airy mix and pin it down so the tiny turtle-patterned leaves root along the stem. Grow it in bright, indirect light and water sparingly, letting the mix dry well between drinks since the succulent leaves store moisture. Let the delicate strands cascade over a small pot or hanging planter.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and pot into small containers.

directions: Root a leaf or stem cutting in moist, well-draining mix and pot the young plant where it gets bright, indirect light to bring out the cream, green, and rosy-red leaf margins. Let the top of the soil dry between waterings, since the thick leaves hold reserves. Pinch back leggy stems to keep this compact plant full and colorful.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in a gritty succulent mix.

directions: Set a stem cutting into a gritty, fast-draining succulent mix and give this semi-succulent peperomia bright, indirect light with a little gentle sun. Its V-shaped leaves show translucent green windows above and deep wine-red beneath, best colored in strong light. Water only once the mix is fully dry, as it rots easily if kept wet.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in spring and pot individually.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in a well-draining mix and grow this peperomia in bright, indirect light for the glossiest teardrop-shaped leaves. Its thick foliage stores water, so let the top half of the pot dry before watering again. Keep it warm and pinch occasionally to maintain a rounded, bushy shape.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in a fast-draining mix.

directions: Set a stem cutting into a gritty, well-draining mix and grow this quirky peperomia in bright, indirect light. Its slender, bean-like succulent leaves have a translucent window along the top edge and store water, so allow the mix to dry between waterings. Avoid overwatering and keep it warm to prevent stem rot.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and pot into small containers.

directions: Root a small cutting in well-draining mix and grow this compact peperomia in bright, indirect light. Its little teardrop leaves stay tidy and only need water once the top of the soil dries. Its miniature size makes it ideal for a desktop or terrarium edge, and pinching keeps it dense.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and pot into a small hanging container.

directions: Root stem cuttings in a well-draining mix and let this trailing hybrid spill from a small pot in bright, indirect light. Its coin-shaped, lightly striped leaves are held in whorls along slender stems that lengthen over time. Water when the top of the soil dries, and pinch the tips to keep the plant full rather than sparse.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings in spring and pot individually.

directions: Root a leaf cutting in moist, well-draining mix and grow this peperomia in bright, indirect light to keep its metallic silver-gray sheen. The quilted, heart-shaped leaves prefer even moisture but never soggy roots, so water when the surface begins to dry. Keep humidity moderate and avoid direct sun, which can scorch the foliage.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and pot into small containers.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in well-draining mix and grow this peperomia in bright, indirect light to highlight the silvery parallel veins striping its dark leaves. Its reddish stems trail and root as they lengthen, so pinch back for a bushier look. Water when the top of the soil dries and keep it warm and out of direct sun.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in a gritty, fast-draining mix.

directions: Set a stem cutting into a gritty, free-draining mix and grow this semi-succulent peperomia in bright light with some gentle direct sun. Its thick, heart-shaped leaves are coated in fine white fuzz that gives the plant a frosted look. Water sparingly once the mix dries, and avoid wetting the felted leaves to prevent rot.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in a succulent mix.

directions: Root a stem cutting in a gritty, fast-draining succulent mix and grow this peperomia in bright, indirect light with a little direct sun. Its folded, purse-shaped leaves have a translucent window along the top edge and store water, so let the mix dry fully between waterings. Keep it warm and err toward dryness to avoid rot.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and pot into small containers.

directions: Root small cuttings in well-draining mix and grow this tiny-leaved peperomia in bright, indirect light to keep the red undersides of its leaves vivid. Its thin, wiry red stems bear whorls of little green leaves and trail attractively. Water when the top of the soil dries and let it spill from a small pot or hang.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in a gritty, fast-draining mix.

directions: Set a short cutting into a gritty succulent mix and grow this miniature peperomia in bright, indirect light. Its stems are densely stacked with tiny folded leaves that show translucent windows, forming a slender green column. Water sparingly once the mix is dry, as its small succulent form is very sensitive to overwatering.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring in bright light to hold the gold color.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in well-draining mix and give this variegated dwarf umbrella tree bright, indirect light to keep its yellow-and-green markings bold. Its glossy, finger-like leaflets radiate from each stalk, and it stays fuller in good light. Water when the top inch of soil dries and pinch the tips to promote a bushy, branching habit.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring for vigorous warm-season growth.

directions: Set a rooted cutting into free-draining mix and grow this large-leaved umbrella tree in bright, indirect light. The 'Amate' selection is prized for its deep-green, disease-resistant foliage and fast, upright growth into a bold indoor tree. Water when the top inch of soil dries and prune the leader to control its considerable height.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring in a stable, warm location.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in well-draining mix and give this elegant plant bright, indirect light in a spot free of drafts. Its narrow, serrated, coppery leaflets are the main attraction, maturing to a broader, greener form on older plants. Keep the soil evenly moist but not wet, and maintain humidity to prevent leaf drop.

planting.note: Pot or plant out in spring once hard frosts have passed.

directions: Set a rooted plant into rich, well-draining soil in a sheltered spot with dappled or partial shade. Unusually cold-tolerant for a schefflera, it can grow outdoors in mild-winter gardens as well as in a cool conservatory. Keep it evenly moist through the growing season and protect young plants from hard freezes with mulch or wrapping.

planting.note: Pot up rooted cuttings in warm spring weather when active growth resumes.

directions: Root a two-node stem cutting in a chunky, fast-draining epiphyte mix set just below the surface, keeping it warm and lightly moist until new leaves appear. Give bright indirect light to hold the creamy-and-pink leaf variegation and water only after the top inch dries. Let the plant become slightly pot-bound, then wait for star-shaped scented flower clusters to form on the older woody spurs, which you should never remove.

planting.note: Start cuttings in spring as light and warmth increase.

directions: Set a cutting with several crinkled, tightly folded leaves shallowly in a gritty mix and let it root slowly, as this cultivar grows more deliberately than the plain species. Water sparingly and allow the pot to dry well between drinks, since the dense curled foliage holds moisture and rots easily. Give bright light and patience; mature ropes reward you with waxy ball-shaped clusters of fragrant flowers on short old spurs.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring when warmth returns.

directions: Insert a rooted stem tip into an airy bark-based mix and keep it warm and humid while it establishes. Unlike its sister cultivar the variegation sits inside the leaf as cream and pink centers, so give strong indirect light to keep the colors vivid. Water when the top inch dries, feed lightly through the growing season, and leave the flowering spurs intact so scented umbels return each year.

planting.note: Best rooted and potted during the warm growing months.

directions: Root a cutting bearing one or two of the round, coin-shaped succulent leaves in a very open mix and water only when nearly dry. This is a forgiving, vigorous climber that appreciates bright light and a trellis or moss pole. When mature it produces domed clusters of pink flowers with darker centers on persistent spurs, so avoid deadheading the flower stalks.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring for fastest establishment.

directions: Lay a length of stem across a gritty mix so the slim, pointed leaves trail over the pot edge and pin it lightly until roots grip. Grow in bright indirect light with regular but restrained watering, allowing partial drying between. Reddish-brown to burgundy fragrant flower umbels appear on trailing stems, and the plant makes an excellent basket subject.

planting.note: Propagate in the warm season to avoid rot on the fine stems.

directions: Root a cluster of the flat, grass-like leaves in an airy epiphyte mix and keep humidity high while the fine roots form. This species prefers steady moisture more than most hoyas but still needs sharp drainage, so water when the surface dries. Single, star-shaped white flowers with a deep red center appear tucked among the foliage, releasing a light lemony scent.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring; this species blooms younger than most hoyas.

directions: Root a cutting in a well-drained mix and grow this as an upright, shrubby hoya rather than a trailer. It flowers readily and often while young, producing showy umbels of swept-back yellow and white flowers that resemble tiny shooting stars. Keep it in bright indirect light with even moisture and it will rebloom repeatedly through the warm months.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings during warm active growth.

directions: Root a trailing stem of the slim, red-margined leaves in a chunky mix and grow it in a hanging pot for best effect. Bright light deepens the reddish leaf edges, while moderate watering with drying between keeps the roots healthy. Dark maroon flowers with paler centers form in tidy rounded clusters and carry a faint fragrance.

planting.note: Root in spring; small-leaved hoyas establish slowly.

directions: Press a section of the fine trailing stems onto a shallow layer of moist bark mix so the tiny silver-flecked spade-shaped leaves make contact and root along the nodes. Keep humidity up and light bright but indirect, watering when the surface just dries. Small clusters of pale greenish-yellow flowers appear slowly, so grow this compact creeper mainly for its mosaic foliage.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm season for quickest branching.

directions: Root a cutting and grow this as a bushy, self-supporting hoya with neat rows of small stiff leaves. It flowers freely from a young age, producing clusters of tiny greenish-yellow flowers with maroon centers that smell strongly of spice, especially in the evening. Give bright light, even moisture, and light feeding to encourage repeat bloom.

planting.note: Best rooted in warm spring conditions.

directions: Root a cutting of the small heart-shaped leaves, which are dusted with silver flecks that intensify in strong light. Grow in a fast-draining mix and water when the top inch dries, giving good airflow to prevent rot on the dense foliage. Rounded clusters of small white, faintly fragrant flowers form on mature spurs during the warm season.

planting.note: Root cuttings during warm active growth.

directions: Root a stem cutting in an open mix and grow in bright light, which brings out the sunset-red stress coloring and prominent leaf veining. This is a relatively fast, free-flowering species that blooms while young. Expect full rounded umbels of pinkish-tan flowers with a light caramel scent, and keep watering even but never soggy.

planting.note: Root in spring; keep cool and airy as this is a highland species.

directions: Root a cutting of the distinctive fishtail-shaped, strongly veined leaves in an airy mix and grow it cooler and more humid than typical hoyas, as it comes from mountain forests. Keep the soil evenly moist but sharply drained and avoid hot, stagnant air. Curved umbels of white flowers with rosy centers hang below the foliage in the warm months.

planting.note: Root in spring and keep cool; this highland species dislikes heat.

directions: Root the slender trailing stems of small round fuzzy leaves in a fine, moisture-retentive but airy mix and maintain high humidity. This cool-growing species is slow and a bit temperamental, so keep it out of direct sun and never let it fully dry or sit wet. Mature plants produce delicate green-white flowers with a pink center that are prized among collectors.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm growing season.

directions: Root a cutting of the large leaves, which are marked with dark sunken veins over a lighter surface, and grow it up a support in warm, humid conditions. Give bright indirect light and water when the top inch dries, as the thick foliage stores moisture. Rounded clusters of fuzzy white to pink flowers with red centers appear on established plants.

planting.note: Root in warm, humid conditions in spring.

directions: Root a cutting of the long leaves that display bold dark-green veins netting a paler center, a pattern that sharpens in bright indirect light. Provide a warm, humid spot, a support to climb, and a chunky mix kept lightly moist but never soggy. Domed clusters of yellow-and-red star flowers form once the plant matures on a trellis.

planting.note: Root in spring and keep warm and very humid.

directions: Root a cutting of the thick, silver-mottled leaves with wavy red-tinged margins in an airy mix, providing steady warmth and high humidity. Water when the surface dries and keep it out of harsh sun, which can bleach the foliage. Fuzzy white flowers with recurved petals and deep red centers appear in rounded clusters on mature stems.

planting.note: Root cuttings during warm active growth.

directions: Root a cutting of the smooth, thick oval leaves in a fast-draining mix and train it up a support in bright warm conditions. This lowland species is a vigorous, reliable grower that tolerates typical home care well. It bears full rounded umbels of pink flowers with a lighter corona, blooming freely once established.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm, humid spring conditions.

directions: Root a stout cutting of the large leaves and give this vigorous climber a strong support, plenty of warmth, and high humidity. Water when the top inch dries and feed regularly through the growing season to fuel its size. It produces some of the largest flowers in the genus, wide reddish to purple stars borne in loose clusters.

planting.note: Root in warm, humid conditions and grow with strong support.

directions: Root a cutting of the large leathery leaves and train this powerful vine up a trellis in a warm, bright, humid location. It needs room and time to mature but rewards patience with enormous pendant umbels of large bell-shaped brownish-red flowers. Water evenly, feed through the warm season, and never let it dry out completely.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm season for best rooting.

directions: Root a cutting of the thick, hairy leaves and provide bright light, warmth, and a support to scramble across. Water when the top inch dries and give good airflow to keep the fleshy foliage sound. Large, waxy, cupped flowers in shades of yellow, cream, or red appear in open clusters, often spotted inside.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm active growth.

directions: Root a cutting of the slender pointed leaves and grow this vigorous vine in bright indirect light with a support to climb. Water when the surface dries and feed lightly while it is in active growth. It flowers readily, producing full rounded clusters of small star flowers in deep red, orange, or pink tones.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm humid spring conditions.

directions: Root a cutting of the strongly veined leaves and train this Javanese climber up a support in bright, warm, humid conditions. Keep the mix lightly moist but sharply drained and feed through the growing season. It bears striking umbels of green-yellow flowers with a deep reddish-purple center, valued by collectors.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm season.

directions: Root a cutting whose long, narrow, folded leaves droop like clusters of green beans and grow it in a hanging pot. Give bright indirect light and moderate water, letting the top inch dry between drinks. Rounded umbels of white flowers with pinkish-red centers form on the trailing stems and carry a sweet fragrance.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm active growth; blooms young.

directions: Root a cutting of the small oval leaves and grow this compact trailer in bright light with even, moderate watering. It flowers freely and young, producing charming clusters of tiny urn-shaped flowers in pink or yellow that release a caramel-like scent. Let the top inch dry between waterings and grow it in a small hanging pot.
